## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (World)
On June 11, 1963, US President John F. Kennedy delivered a massive, incredibly historic, and heavily televised national address on civil rights. Prompted aggressively by the massive, highly publicized integration of the University of Alabama by National Guard troops, Kennedy famously and aggressively declared civil rights an absolute "moral issue." This monumental speech heavily laid the absolute, foundational political groundwork for the massive Civil Rights Act of 1964, fundamentally committing the federal government to aggressively dismantling systemic racial segregation.

On June 11, 2001 (Historical context note), Timothy McVeigh, the massive, deeply radicalized anti-government terrorist who perpetrated the horrific Oklahoma City bombing, was officially and aggressively executed by lethal injection at a federal penitentiary in Indiana. His massive, incredibly devastating attack in 1995 killed 168 people and remained the absolute, deadliest act of domestic terrorism in US history, deeply highlighting the massive, continuing threat of heavily armed, right-wing extremist militias within the United States.

On June 11, 1897, Ram Prasad Bismil, the incredibly massive, deeply revered, and fiercely militant revolutionary of the Indian independence movement, was officially born in Shahjahanpur. On June 11, 1999, during the massive, incredibly brutal heights of the Kargil War, the Indian Armed Forces aggressively achieved a massive, strategic military success.
